hub_subtitle: Inglês para Trabalho Remoto em Tecnologia & Quality Assurance

lesson.menu_desc: Ana joins a requirements call with a product owner to clarify a new feature before she tests it -- asking direct and indirect questions and confirming the scope, constraints and acceptance criteria -- 33 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 9 -- Talking About Requirements
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Ana Claudia Veraldi | Lesson 9 -- Talking About Requirements

listenings.text: Welcome to the team. Let me share how we handle requirements here. The most important skill is not writing code; it is asking good questions. When you get a new feature, never assume. First, read the specification, but remember it is often ambiguous, so you have to clarify it. Ask what the main use case is, and who the end user really is. Then confirm the scope: what is included, and, just as important, what is not. Always check the constraints early, like the deadline or the file size, because they decide what is feasible. And when someone gives you an answer, repeat it back to confirm you understood. A good analyst gathers the requirements by asking, listening, and double-checking. If you do that, you test the right thing, and you save everyone a lot of time.

Hi Ana, it is Carla from the product team. I am leaving you a quick message about the export feature, because the ticket was a little ambiguous. So, to be clear about the requirements: the end user must be able to export both the charts and the tables, not just one of them. The file has to be a PDF, and it must stay under ten megabytes; that is a firm constraint. One assumption in the ticket is wrong, by the way: the report does not need to work offline, so please ignore that part. If anything is still unclear, just double-check with me before you write the test cases. I would rather confirm now than fix a bug later. Thanks, Ana.

extra_audio.text: Here is how Ana clarifies a new requirement. First, she reads the specification for the feature. Then she asks what the main use case is and who the end user is. Next, she confirms the scope: what is included and what is not. After that, she checks the constraints, like the deadline and the file size. Finally, she repeats everything back to confirm she understood.
